title: It's OK That You're Not OK

chapters.title: Grief proves how deeply we loved
chapters.text:
No shortcut exists for the ache of losing someone cherished. What we can learn is how to tend that wound. Grief delivers a particular wisdom available only by walking through loss. If we stop fleeing uncomfortable feelings, we gain insight that love and bereavement together provide.

Culture teaches us to slap a bandage over mourning, yet grief can train us toward wholeness. Megan Devine, a psychotherapist with ten years of clinical practice, discovered this after her husband died suddenly. Expertise in counseling others couldn't prepare her for living inside loss. Standard grief frameworks failed; she began sharing what the experience actually taught.

Because love and loss intertwine, we must weave sorrow into a life that honors both.

Devine's husband drowned in 2009, three days before his fortieth birthday. The tragedy exposed how her profession — and society — mishandle bereavement. The world dismisses, judges, and misunderstands grievers, often without malice. She developed a more humane way to meet grief and support each other through it.

This summary shows how to restore grief's dignity as a normal life passage, then outlines skills for navigating the change loss demands.

Grief isn't illness requiring cure. We need compassion and practical tools for the journey. To love well during devastation, we first must face grief honestly.

chapters.title: Grievers need company, not fixes
chapters.text:
Our culture treats grief like a bug to debug. Well-meaning words often deny or shrink the loss, deepening isolation.

There is nothing wrong with grief. It's a natural extension of love. It's a healthy and sane response to loss. ~ Megan Devine

Some pain transforms; some simply is. Slogans can't erase it — they worsen matters by rejecting reality or shrinking the magnitude of what happened.

Living inside loss with grace begins with telling the truth.

First understand our emotional illiteracy and cultural allergy to pain. Then explore how grief reshapes body and spirit. Choose supporters carefully. Finally, chart a path where love and loss coexist.

We inhabit a world that treats discomfort as error, so helpers rush grievers toward "normal" as fast as possible.

Switch to truth mode: some losses can't be redeemed, lack reason, and carry no silver lining. Admit that loving each other guarantees losing each other. Only then can we learn to inhabit absence instead of treating loss as catastrophe to avoid.

Survival isn't procedural. Grief has no stages — our vocabulary gap stems from cultural roots. Others project foreign narratives onto mourners. Refusing invalidation won't fix the loss, but it changes everything about carrying it.

chapters.title: Comfort words often deepen the wound
chapters.text:
Friends and acquaintances want to help yet lack language. Often silence beats hollow reassurance. Most comfort phrases minimize pain, leaving helpers frustrated when solace backfires.

Would-be comforters fail because they see grief as malfunction instead of natural response.

Sharing parallel loss stories seems supportive, yet each bereavement mirrors the unique love preceding it. Your husband's death doesn't mean you grasp this specific absence. Stop claiming you do. Stories may help later, at the right moment; in early grief they spark pain competitions about who hurts more.

Every loss deserves honor; comparing grief forms is wrong.

Comparison doesn't heal. Another trap: phrases that shrink suffering. "She wouldn't want you to be sad" hides an implied command — "so stop feeling bad" — twisting kindness into criticism.

Some comforts insist the loss served a higher purpose. Lessons might arrive through countless routes — you didn't need this tragedy to grow.

You can build wholeness atop grief by choice, not because loss was necessary. Claiming you needed death to mature suggests prior powerlessness or guilt — which is why comfort talk often triggers self-doubt.

chapters.title: Cultural grief illiteracy compounds pain
chapters.text:
Therapists often pathologize mourning — treating prolonged sorrow as disorder that shouldn't linger. Happiness reads healthy; sadness reads sick. Language designed to hurry you toward joy frequently achieves the opposite.

Doctors label grief persisting beyond six months as clinical disorder.

Misdiagnosis partly stems from Elisabeth Kübler-Ross's five-stage model in her 1969 book On Death and Dying. Kübler-Ross later condemned turning her framework into a linear universal timeline. She aimed to validate grief's diversity, not sequence it. Manifestations vary person to person — there are no official stages.

Other forces breed grief illiteracy: solution-obsessed messaging and entertainment's redemption arcs. Films and songs convert tragedy into "useful" transformation; heroes rebound happy after enormous loss. We fetishize struggle to celebrate tidy endings. Brené Brown, author of Rising Strong, calls this "a Gilded Age of Failure."

Grievers who don't perform the comeback narrative look abnormal. We need cultural stories admitting some grief can't be redeemed — only carried.

chapters.title: Witness grief without demanding recovery
chapters.text:
We've traced how avoidance culture gags honest mourning. Proper tending means finding middle ground between denial and doom — experiencing grief's full span as love's extension. Witnessing grief means embracing mystery instead of mastering it.

Allowing grief room reveals tender ways to care for it. Stop fighting pain to reclaim freedom to love and reduce suffering. Self-compassion extends outward. Pain's reality stays fixed, yet love's expression deepens when loss joins the story.

Express feelings without defending them when judged. Supporters relax once they know their job isn't fixing you — only helping carry weight.

Speaking grief aloud inside an emotionally illiterate culture is fierce self-love.

Right tools chart your middle path — no pretending mastery, no abandoning life because hurt persists.

Grief arrives early or late. Many resources address later mourning; few speak to razor-sharp early days. Seek what works now.

Early grief steals language. Words vanish. Acknowledge current limits without shame.

chapters.title: Naming the loss anchors you in reality
chapters.text:
Admitting what happened creates footing during chaos. Funerals, burials, eulogies, and unavoidable obligations pile up — delegate what others can handle; keep intimate tasks if they matter to you.

Telling your story aloud or on paper orders a scattered mind.

Land mines of memory wait everywhere. Take breaks from people as long as needed; sometimes a temporary environment change helps. Practice self-kindness. Ignore timelines for returning to "normal."

Use the vomit metric: if donating a child's clothes or removing a wedding ring triggers nausea or hives, wait.

Everyone grieves differently, and everyone has a different way they memorialize or acknowledge the person who is gone. ~ Megan Devine

You're not obliged to stay at memorials you organized if discomfort spikes. Respect others' paths — they may skip services or mourn privately. Children grieve differently; discuss death, pain, and love in age-appropriate terms.

Family fractures happen. Anger is valid. Treat emotions as responses to events; suppressed anger pressurizes into toxicity. Find an ally — therapist or friend — who can hear rage without flinching.

Did you know? Extending your exhale beyond your inhale can calm fight-or-flight arousal.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Love reduces suffering when we connect pain to a loving heart instead of letting pain swallow us. Suffering arrives when hurt gets dismissed or judged; pain itself is healthy response to losing someone treasured.

Unexpressed pain backlog creates further harm. Tend it rather than correcting or containing it. In grief, experiment for relief — track stressors and what brings relative calm. Self-compassion, boundary-setting, validation, and rest signal wellness.

Supporting someone grieving? Don't steal their mourning with unsolicited advice. Stay present with pain instead of dragging them toward past or future. Say simply: "This hurts. I love you. I'm here."

Resist fixing. Focus on reducing suffering — this isn't about you. Witness instead of rescuing. Offer concrete help rather than "call if you need me," and follow through.

Ultimately: show up, speak honestly, act. Listen more; educate others on better support.

Try this

Truth plus love eases suffering. In early grief, experiment and log what helps versus harms; tend pain by prioritizing what works.

In later grief, seek orientation, not mastery. Open to love that honors the bond — because loss extends love's reach.

Supporting someone else? Guard your tongue; refuse to shrink or judge their grief.

Read it till the end?
